body:
## Overview
Currently Notion IDs are hardcoded in the codebase. This blocks multi-user adoption and violates separation of concerns. Need to move all workspace-specific values to user configuration.

## Background
Discovery during Friday 8/29 cleanup work revealed mixing of PIPER system-level and user-level configuration. Proper separation ensures system functionality remains independent of individual user preferences while allowing appropriate customization.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All hardcoded Notion IDs identified and documented
- [ ] Configuration schema designed and implemented (with ADR-027)
- [ ] Code refactored to use configuration
- [ ] Backward compatibility maintained
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] Full test coverage passing

## Implementation Phases
Links to sub-issues will be added as they are created:
- Phase 1: Audit codebase for hardcoded values
- Phase 2: Design configuration schema  
- Phase 3: Implement configuration loader
- Phase 4: Refactor commands to use config
- Phase 5: Test and document changes

## Risk Mitigation
- Backup current working state before refactoring
- Test after each phase to catch issues early
- Maintain backward compatibility throughout

### Implementation Approach - DocumentService Extension
**ARCHITECTURE DECISION**: Extended existing DocumentService instead of DocumentMemoryQueries

## 🎯 Objective

Transform the overwhelming 609-line README.md into a user-friendly three-tier documentation architecture that reduces cognitive load for newcomers while maintaining transparency and comprehensive information access.

## 📊 Problem Analysis

### Current Issues
- **609 lines** - Far too long for newcomers  
- **No table of contents** - Impossible to navigate
- **Mixed audiences** - Product managers, developers, and users all jumbled together
- **Outdated status claims** - PM-033b listed as latest achievement (July timeframe)
- **Missing recent work** - No mention of Issue Intelligence (PM-121, PM-122, PM-123), Excellence Flywheel implementation
- **Cognitive overload** - 15 major sections with 73 checklist items
- **Multiple "Getting Started" sections** create confusion

## 🏗️ Solution: Three-Tier Architecture

### New Structure
1. **README.md** (~200 lines max)
   - Clear value proposition
   - Single demo workflow  
   - User-type specific paths
   - Current quarter focus only
   - Brief architecture overview

2. **docs/getting-started/** (NEW)
   - User-type specific guides (PM, Dev, Admin)
   - Comprehensive setup instructions
   - Environment configuration details

3. **docs/status/** (NEW) 
   - Detailed achievements with PM numbers
   - Historical completions
   - Forward-looking roadmap
   - Comprehensive changelog

4. **docs/README.md** (NEW)
   - Master documentation navigation hub
   - Comprehensive link directory

## 📋 Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Foundation ✅
- [ ] Create new directory structure  
- [ ] Create placeholder files for new documentation pages
- [ ] Migrate content to appropriate locations
- [ ] Update internal links

### Phase 2: Content Creation ✅
- [ ] Write getting-started guides for different user types
- [ ] Create comprehensive status dashboard  
- [ ] Write master documentation hub
- [ ] Craft focused new README

### Phase 3: Integration & Testing ✅
- [ ] Update all cross-references
- [ ] Test all links work properly
- [ ] Verify new user journey flows

### Phase 4: Excellence Flywheel Completion ✅
- [ ] Update backlog.md and CSV tracking
- [ ] Test functionality thoroughly
- [ ] Update session logs
- [ ] Mark issue complete with evidence

## 🎯 Success Criteria

### New User Experience
- [ ] README under 200 lines
- [ ] Clear user paths within 30 seconds of landing  
- [ ] Focused value proposition without technical overwhelm

### Maintainability  
- [ ] Capability-driven status updates (quarterly refresh)
- [ ] Separation of concerns (user vs. developer content)
- [ ] No more micro-updates for individual tickets

### Information Architecture
- [ ] Logical content hierarchy with clear entry points
- [ ] Zero broken links maintained through restructure
- [ ] All comprehensive documentation remains discoverable

## 🔄 New Update Philosophy

**"Capability-Driven, Not Ticket-Driven"**
- README announces major capabilities when user-ready
- Quarterly updates to status section
- Focus on user value over implementation details  
- Historical completions in dedicated changelog/status pages

## 📚 Files to Create/Modify

### NEW FILES
- `docs/getting-started/README.md` - Master getting started index
- `docs/getting-started/product-managers.md` - PM-specific quick start
- `docs/getting-started/developers.md` - Full dev environment setup
- `docs/getting-started/api-integration.md` - API consumer setup  
- `docs/getting-started/production.md` - Production deployment guide
- `docs/status/README.md` - Current status dashboard
- `docs/status/achievements-2025.md` - All 2025 completions

**Priority**: P0 - Critical

## Problem Statement

Architecture audit found legacy DatabasePool class and deprecated RepositoryFactory that violate current AsyncSessionFactory standards and provide anti-pattern implementations.

## Current State
- `services/repositories/__init__.py` contains DatabasePool class using direct asyncpg
- `services/database/repositories.py` contains deprecated RepositoryFactory class  
- `services/database/__init__.py` exports RepositoryFactory in module `__all__`
- These classes bypass SQLAlchemy entirely and enable anti-pattern usage
- Creates confusion and enables continued use of deprecated patterns

## Target State
- Remove DatabasePool class entirely from repositories/__init__.py
- Remove deprecated RepositoryFactory class from repositories.py
- Remove RepositoryFactory from database module exports
- Clean architecture with only AsyncSessionFactory as session management

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Delete DatabasePool class from `services/repositories/__init__.py`
- [ ] Delete RepositoryFactory class from `services/database/repositories.py`
- [ ] Remove RepositoryFactory from `services/database/__init__.py` exports
- [ ] Verify no active code imports these deprecated classes
- [ ] Update any remaining imports (none found in audit but verify)
- [ ] Ensure tests don't rely on removed classes

## Technical Context

**Files Requiring Changes:**
- `services/repositories/__init__.py` - Remove DatabasePool class
- `services/database/repositories.py` - Remove RepositoryFactory class  
- `services/database/__init__.py` - Remove from __all__ exports

**Anti-Pattern Elimination:**
- DatabasePool bypasses SQLAlchemy ORM entirely
- RepositoryFactory marked deprecated but still available
- Both enable non-standard session management patterns

## Risk Assessment
**Medium Risk** - Removing deprecated classes
**Impact**: Cleanup of deprecated architecture components
**Mitigation**: Audit confirmed no active usage in production code

## Definition of Done
- DatabasePool and RepositoryFactory classes completely removed
- No exports of deprecated session management classes
- AsyncSessionFactory remains as sole session management pattern
- All tests pass after removal
- Clean architecture with no deprecated patterns available

**Priority**: P0 - Critical

**Component**: Core Application (main.py)

## Problem Statement

Architecture audit revealed that main.py is using deprecated DatabasePool pattern instead of the standardized AsyncSessionFactory pattern established in ADR-006 and ADR-012.

## Current State
- `main.py` uses `DatabasePool.get_pool()` for database connections
- IntentEnricher accepts raw `db` parameter instead of AsyncSession
- Violates established session management standards
- Active production code using anti-pattern

## Target State
- Replace DatabasePool usage with AsyncSessionFactory.session_scope()
- Update IntentEnricher constructor to accept AsyncSession parameter
- Ensure proper session lifecycle management in request handlers
- Full compliance with ADR-006/ADR-012 session management standards

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Remove DatabasePool.get_pool() usage from main.py
- [ ] Implement AsyncSessionFactory.session_scope() pattern
- [ ] Update IntentEnricher to accept AsyncSession dependency injection
- [ ] Verify proper session lifecycle management (auto-cleanup)
- [ ] Ensure no regression in database functionality
- [ ] Update any related tests to use new pattern

## Technical Context

**Files Requiring Changes:**
- `main.py` - Replace DatabasePool with AsyncSessionFactory
- `services/intent_service/intent_enricher.py` - Update dependency injection

**Reference Architecture:**
- ADR-006: Standardize Async Session Management
- ADR-012: Unified Session Management
- `services/database/session_factory.py` - Proper AsyncSessionFactory implementation

## Risk Assessment
**High Risk** - Core application database connectivity
**Impact**: Production database session management
**Mitigation**: Thorough testing of session lifecycle and cleanup

## Definition of Done
- All database operations in main.py use AsyncSessionFactory pattern
- IntentEnricher follows proper dependency injection with AsyncSession
- No remaining DatabasePool usage in production code
- Tests pass with new session management pattern
- Documentation updated if needed

---

**Discovered by**: AsyncSessionFactory Architecture Audit (2025-08-18)
**Related Issues**: Part of systematic architecture compliance initiative

```python
class ProjectPriorityLearner:

        Learning Patterns:
        - User consistently spends more time on Project X than recommended → Increase Project X priority
        - User avoids Project Y despite high recommendations → Investigate barriers or reduce priority
        - User follows recommendations on Project Z → Validation of current priority calculation
        - Seasonal patterns in project focus → Update priority weights based on time/context
```

## Priority Algorithm Refinement
### Feedback-Based Weight Adjustment
```python
@dataclass
class PriorityFeedback:
    recommendation_id: str
    user_response: UserResponseType  # FOLLOWED, IGNORED, MODIFIED, REJECTED
    stated_reason: Optional[str]
    actual_outcome: Optional[str]
    satisfaction_rating: Optional[float]  # 1-5 scale
    
class PriorityAlgorithmRefiner:
    def refine_priority_weights(self, 
                              feedback_history: List[PriorityFeedback],
                              current_weights: Dict[str, float]) -> Dict[str, float]:
        """
        Refine priority calculation weights based on feedback:
        
        Weight Adjustment Logic:
        - High satisfaction with deadline-driven recommendations → Increase deadline weight
        - User frequently ignores dependency-based priorities → Decrease dependency weight
        - Strategic alignment recommendations well-received → Increase goal alignment weight
        - Efficiency-based recommendations consistently rejected → Decrease efficiency weight
        
        Learning Rate: Conservative adjustment (5-10% per iteration) to prevent over-fitting
        """
```

## Personality Framework Components

### Response Enhancement Patterns
#### Professional but Warm Tone
```python
class ToneEnhancer:
    def apply_warm_professional_tone(self, response: str) -> str:
        """
        Transform technical responses to warm but professional:
        
        Before: "The system indicates 3 active projects with priority scores."
        After: "Great question\! I can see you're juggling 3 active projects right now."
        
        Before: "Database query returned no results for the specified parameters."  
        After: "I don't see any matching results for that, but let me suggest a few alternatives."
        
        Techniques:

        - Replace system language with human language

        - Maintain professional accuracy while adding warmth
```

#### Confidence Score Integration
```python
@dataclass
class ConfidenceMetrics:

    calculation_certainty: float  # Accuracy of calculations/algorithms

    def format_confidence_indicator(self, 
                                  confidence: ConfidenceMetrics,
                                  response_type: ResponseType) -> str:
        """
```

## Description
Develop algorithm for priority scoring that considers deadlines, dependencies, impact, and Q4 goal alignment to enable accurate responses to "What's my top priority?" queries.

## Problem Statement
Current system lacks intelligent priority calculation:
- No systematic algorithm for ranking tasks and projects by importance
- Missing consideration of deadlines, dependencies, and business impact
- Incomplete alignment with Q4 goals and strategic objectives  
- Inability to provide definitive answers to "What should I focus on?" queries

## Solution: Multi-Factor Priority Calculation Engine
Build comprehensive priority scoring system:
- Weighted algorithm considering deadlines, dependencies, impact, and goal alignment
- Real-time priority recalculation based on changing conditions
- Context-aware prioritization based on available time and energy
- Strategic alignment scoring with Q4 objectives and business goals

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Multi-factor priority scoring algorithm implemented
- [ ] Deadline proximity weighting (closer deadlines = higher priority)
- [ ] Dependency analysis (blockers and blocked items prioritized)
- [ ] Business impact scoring (revenue, user experience, strategic value)
- [ ] Q4 goal alignment scoring (strategic objective contribution)
- [ ] Context-aware prioritization (time available, energy level, meeting schedule)
- [ ] "What's my top priority?" answered with definitive, justified ranking
- [ ] Priority recalculation triggers on project/deadline changes

## Technical Implementation
```python
# services/intelligence/priority_calculation_engine.py
class PriorityCalculationEngine:
    def __init__(self, 
                 portfolio_analyzer: ProjectPortfolioAnalyzer,
                 temporal_service: TemporalContextService,
                 goal_alignment_service: GoalAlignmentService):
        self.portfolio = portfolio_analyzer  
        self.temporal = temporal_service
        self.goal_alignment = goal_alignment_service

                                      context: PriorityContext) -> List[PriorityScore]:

    async def get_top_priority_recommendation(self, 
                                            available_time_minutes: int) -> PriorityRecommendation:
        """Get definitive top priority recommendation with justification"""
```

## Priority Scoring Algorithm
### Multi-Factor Weighted Scoring (Total = 1.0)
```python
class PriorityScorer:
    def calculate_priority_score(self, item: PriorityItem) -> PriorityScore:
        """
        Weighted priority calculation:
        
        1. Deadline Proximity (25% weight)
           - Days until deadline vs. estimated completion time
           - Exponential urgency curve for approaching deadlines
           
        2. Dependency Impact (20% weight)  
           - Number of items blocked by this item
           - Critical path analysis in project dependency graph
           
        3. Business Impact (20% weight)
           - Revenue impact (direct/indirect)
           - User experience improvement score
           - Strategic business value rating
           
        4. Q4 Goal Alignment (20% weight)
           - Direct contribution to Q4 objectives
           - Key result advancement potential
           - Strategic initiative alignment
           
        5. Resource Efficiency (15% weight)
           - Time investment vs. impact ratio
           - Complexity/effort required
           - Available skill alignment
        """
        
        deadline_score = self._calculate_deadline_urgency(item)
        dependency_score = self._calculate_dependency_impact(item)  
        impact_score = self._calculate_business_impact(item)
        alignment_score = self._calculate_goal_alignment(item)
        efficiency_score = self._calculate_resource_efficiency(item)
        
        total_score = (
            deadline_score * 0.25 +
            dependency_score * 0.20 +
            impact_score * 0.20 +  
            alignment_score * 0.20 +
            efficiency_score * 0.15
        )
        
        return PriorityScore(item=item, score=total_score, factors={...})
```

## Deadline Proximity Calculation
### Urgency Curve Algorithm
```python
def _calculate_deadline_urgency(self, item: PriorityItem) -> float:
    """
    Calculate deadline urgency with exponential curve:
    
    Days Remaining | Urgency Score
    30+ days       | 0.1 (low urgency)
    14-30 days     | 0.3 (moderate)
    7-14 days      | 0.6 (high)
    3-7 days       | 0.8 (urgent)
    1-3 days       | 0.95 (critical)
    Overdue        | 1.0 (maximum)
    """
    days_until_deadline = (item.deadline - datetime.now()).days
    estimated_completion_days = item.estimated_hours / 8  # Assume 8 hours/day
    
    # Adjust for completion time vs. available time
    urgency_ratio = estimated_completion_days / max(days_until_deadline, 1)
    
    # Exponential urgency curve
    if days_until_deadline < 0:  # Overdue
        return 1.0
    elif days_until_deadline <= 1:
        return 0.95
    elif days_until_deadline <= 3:
        return 0.8
    elif days_until_deadline <= 7:
        return 0.6
    elif days_until_deadline <= 14:
        return 0.3
    else:
        return 0.1
```

## Dependency Impact Analysis
### Critical Path and Blocker Analysis
```python
def _calculate_dependency_impact(self, item: PriorityItem) -> float:
    """
    Calculate impact based on dependency relationships:
    
    - Items blocking multiple others: Higher priority
    - Items on critical path: Elevated priority
    - Items with no dependencies: Lower base priority
    - Circular dependencies: Special handling required
    """
    blocked_items_count = len(item.blocks)
    is_on_critical_path = self.dependency_analyzer.is_critical_path(item)
    dependency_chain_length = self.dependency_analyzer.get_chain_length(item)
    
    base_score = min(blocked_items_count * 0.2, 0.8)  # Cap at 0.8
    critical_path_bonus = 0.2 if is_on_critical_path else 0
    
    return min(base_score + critical_path_bonus, 1.0)
```

## Business Impact Scoring
### Strategic Value Assessment
```python
@dataclass
class BusinessImpact:
    revenue_impact: float  # 0.0-1.0 scale
    user_experience_impact: float
    strategic_value: float
    risk_mitigation_value: float
    
def _calculate_business_impact(self, item: PriorityItem) -> float:
    """
    Assess business impact across multiple dimensions:
    
    Revenue Impact (40%):
    - Direct revenue generation or protection
    - Customer retention and acquisition
    - Market positioning advantages
    
    User Experience (30%):
    - User satisfaction improvements
    - Usability enhancements
    - Feature adoption potential
    
    Strategic Value (20%):
    - Long-term competitive advantage
    - Platform/infrastructure foundation
    - Technical debt reduction
    
    Risk Mitigation (10%):
    - Security vulnerability fixes
    - Compliance requirements
    - Operational stability improvements
    """
```

## Q4 Goal Alignment Scoring  
### Strategic Objective Mapping
```python
class Q4GoalAlignmentScorer:
    def __init__(self, q4_objectives: List[StrategicObjective]):
        self.q4_objectives = q4_objectives
        
    def calculate_alignment_score(self, item: PriorityItem) -> float:
        """
        Score alignment with Q4 strategic objectives:
        
        Q4 2025 Objectives:
        1. Establish Piper as premier AI PM assistant (40% weight)
        2. Achieve market-ready product stability (25% weight)  
        3. Build sustainable user acquisition (20% weight)
        4. Establish technical foundation for scale (15% weight)
        """
        alignment_scores = []
        
        for objective in self.q4_objectives:
            item_contribution = self._assess_contribution(item, objective)
            weighted_contribution = item_contribution * objective.weight
            alignment_scores.append(weighted_contribution)
            
        return sum(alignment_scores)
```

## Context-Aware Prioritization
### Available Time and Energy Consideration
```python
class ContextualPrioritizer:
    async def adjust_priorities_for_context(self, 
                                          base_priorities: List[PriorityScore],
                                          context: WorkContext) -> List[PriorityScore]:
```

## Solution: Proactive Calendar Intelligence
Automatically scan and analyze calendar on greeting detection:

- Identify key meetings, conflicts, and dependencies
- Surface proactive insights and recommendations in greeting response

- [ ] Detect schedule conflicts and double-bookings
- [ ] Surface dependencies between meetings and project work
- [ ] Proactive greeting response with schedule insights
- [ ] Performance target: Calendar scan completes in <500ms
- [ ] Graceful handling if calendar unavailable

```python
                 calendar_service: CalendarService):

                                                 insights: CalendarInsights) -> str:
```

- **Formal**: "Hello Piper", "Good day"

## Description
Parse GitHub/MCP data for active projects, calculate time allocations, and track project status to enable accurate responses to "What am I working on?" queries.

## Problem Statement
Current system lacks comprehensive project portfolio awareness:
- No automatic discovery of active projects from GitHub/MCP sources
- Missing time allocation calculations across multiple projects
- Incomplete project status tracking and progress awareness
- Inability to answer "What am I working on?" with accurate, current information

## Solution: Automated Project Portfolio Analysis
Integrate with existing MCP Consumer (PM-033a complete ✅) and GitHub data to:
- Automatically discover active projects from repositories and issues
- Calculate time allocation percentages based on recent activity
- Track project status, progress, and current phase
- Provide comprehensive portfolio overview for standup queries

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Parse GitHub repositories for active project identification
- [ ] Integrate with MCP Consumer to gather cross-platform project data
- [ ] Calculate time allocation percentages based on recent commits, issues, PRs
- [ ] Track project status (active, blocked, completed, on-hold)
- [ ] Determine current project phases and next milestones
- [ ] Answer "What am I working on?" with accurate portfolio breakdown
- [ ] Provide project priority rankings based on recent activity
- [ ] Performance target: Portfolio analysis completes in <200ms

## Technical Implementation
```python
# services/portfolio/project_portfolio_analyzer.py
class ProjectPortfolioAnalyzer:
    def __init__(self, github_adapter: GitHubMCPSpatialAdapter, 
                 mcp_consumer: MCPConsumerCore):
        self.github_adapter = github_adapter
        self.mcp_consumer = mcp_consumer
        
    async def discover_active_projects(self) -> List[ProjectSummary]:
        """Discover active projects from GitHub and MCP sources"""
        
    async def calculate_time_allocations(self, 
                                       timeframe_days: int = 30) -> Dict[str, float]:
        """Calculate percentage time allocation per project"""
        
    async def analyze_project_status(self, project: str) -> ProjectStatus:
        """Analyze current project status and phase"""
        
    async def get_portfolio_overview(self) -> PortfolioOverview:
        """Get comprehensive portfolio overview for standup queries"""
```

## Data Sources Integration
### GitHub Data (via MCP Consumer PM-033a ✅)
- Repository activity (commits, PRs, issues)
- Issue creation, updates, and resolution patterns
- Milestone progress and completion rates
- Branch activity and merge patterns

### Cross-Platform MCP Data
- Linear issues and project management data  
- Documentation updates and knowledge base changes
- CI/CD pipeline activity and deployment frequency
- Time tracking data from integrated tools

### Activity Analysis Algorithm
```python
class ActivityAnalyzer:
    def calculate_project_allocation(self, activities: List[Activity]) -> Dict[str, float]:
        """
        Calculate time allocation based on:
        - Commit frequency and size (40% weight)
        - Issue/PR activity (30% weight)  
        - Documentation updates (20% weight)
        - Meeting/calendar time (10% weight)
        """
```

## Project Status Classification
### Status Categories
- **ACTIVE**: Recent activity (last 7 days), in progress
- **FOCUSED**: High activity concentration (>40% time allocation)
- **MAINTENANCE**: Low but consistent activity (weekly commits/updates)
- **BLOCKED**: No recent activity but open critical issues
- **COMPLETED**: Closed milestone, archived, or tagged complete
- **ON_HOLD**: Explicitly marked or no activity >30 days

### Progress Tracking
```python
@dataclass
class ProjectStatus:
    name: str
    status: ProjectStatusType
    current_phase: str
    progress_percentage: float
    time_allocation_percentage: float
    next_milestone: Optional[str]
    blockers: List[str]
    recent_activity: List[Activity]
```

## Description
Add TEMPORAL, STATUS, and PRIORITY intent categories to fix "Failed to process intent" errors in canonical queries. This foundational work enables proper intent recognition for conversational standup queries.

## Problem Statement
Current intent classifier lacks conversational intent categories needed for natural standup interactions:
- **TEMPORAL intents**: "What day is it?", "What's my schedule today?"
- **STATUS intents**: "What am I working on?", "Show me current projects"  
- **PRIORITY intents**: "What's my top priority?", "What should I focus on?"

These missing categories cause "Failed to process intent" errors for canonical queries.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Add TEMPORAL intent category with patterns for time/date/schedule queries
- [ ] Add STATUS intent category with patterns for current work/project queries
- [ ] Add PRIORITY intent category with patterns for priority/focus queries
- [ ] Update intent classifier training data with new categories
- [ ] Test with all 5 canonical queries - must recognize intents correctly
- [ ] All canonical queries show proper intent classification (no "Failed to process" errors)
- [ ] Intent confidence scores >0.8 for canonical queries

## Technical Implementation
```python
# services/shared_types.py - Add new intent categories
class IntentCategory(Enum):
    # Existing categories...
    TEMPORAL = "temporal"      # NEW: Time/date/schedule queries
    STATUS = "status"          # NEW: Current work/project status  
    PRIORITY = "priority"      # NEW: Priority/focus queries
```

**TEMPORAL Intents:**
- "What day is it?"
- "What's my schedule today?"
- "When is my next meeting?"

**STATUS Intents:**
- "What am I working on?"
- "Show me my current projects"

**PRIORITY Intents:**
- "What's my top priority?"
- "What should I focus on?"
- "What's most important today?"

## Testing Strategy
1. **Unit Tests**: Verify new intent categories added to enum

## Description
Implement comprehensive multi-agent coordination system with task decomposition, parallel deployment capabilities, and Excellence Flywheel enforcement. This transforms Piper Morgan into a coordination hub that can orchestrate multiple AI agents (Code + Cursor) simultaneously while maintaining systematic quality standards.

## 🎯 Multi-Agent Coordination Vision
**Goal**: Enable sophisticated multi-agent coordination with:
- Intelligent task decomposition and agent assignment
- Parallel deployment (Code + Cursor simultaneously)
- Excellence Flywheel methodology enforcement across agents
- Kind communication wrapper for seamless human interactions
- Simple 2-draft Chain-of-Draft experimentation framework
- Performance optimization with <1000ms coordination overhead

## Acceptance Criteria

### Core Multi-Agent Coordination
- [ ] **Task Decomposition Engine**: Automatically break complex tasks into agent-specific assignments
- [ ] **Parallel Agent Deployment**: Deploy Code + Cursor agents simultaneously with clear role separation
- [ ] **Agent Strength Assignment**: Code (infrastructure/backend), Cursor (testing/UI/polish)
- [ ] **Coordination Protocol**: Seamless handoff and collaboration between agents
- [ ] **Conflict Resolution**: Handle overlapping work areas and merge conflicts

### Excellence Flywheel Enforcement
- [ ] **Systematic Verification**: All agents follow "Verify First, Implement Second" methodology
- [ ] **Quality Gates**: Mandatory pattern discovery before implementation
- [ ] **Consistency Enforcement**: Architectural patterns maintained across all agent work
- [ ] **Progress Tracking**: Real-time visibility into multi-agent work streams

### Kind Communication Integration
- [ ] **Human-Friendly Interface**: Kind communication wrapper for all multi-agent interactions
- [ ] **Status Translation**: Technical progress translated into human-readable updates
- [ ] **Decision Points**: Clear human approval gates for major architectural decisions
- [ ] **Error Communication**: Graceful error handling with kind explanations

### Chain-of-Draft Experiment
- [ ] **2-Draft Framework**: Simple experimentation with iterative improvement cycles
- [ ] **Draft Comparison**: Side-by-side comparison of different implementation approaches
- [ ] **Learning Integration**: Capture and apply learnings from draft iterations
- [ ] **Performance Metrics**: Measure improvement between draft iterations

### Performance Requirements
- [ ] **Coordination Latency**: <1000ms overhead for multi-agent task coordination
- [ ] **Agent Startup Time**: <30s to deploy and initialize agent pair
- [ ] **Communication Efficiency**: Minimal overhead for inter-agent communication
- [ ] **Resource Management**: Efficient allocation of computational resources

## Technical Architecture

### Multi-Agent Coordination Engine
```python
class MultiAgentCoordinator:
    """Orchestrates multiple AI agents with task decomposition and coordination"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.task_decomposer = TaskDecomposer()
        self.agent_registry = AgentRegistry()
        self.coordination_protocol = CoordinationProtocol()
        self.quality_enforcer = ExcellenceFlywheel()
        self.kind_communicator = KindCommunicationWrapper()
        
    async def coordinate_task(self, task: ComplexTask) -> CoordinationResult:
        # 1. Decompose task into agent-specific subtasks
        subtasks = await self.task_decomposer.decompose(task)
        
        # 2. Assign tasks based on agent strengths
        assignments = await self.assign_to_agents(subtasks)
        
        # 3. Deploy agents in parallel
        results = await self.deploy_parallel(assignments)
        
        # 4. Coordinate and merge results
        return await self.merge_results(results)
```

### Agent Strength Assignments
- **Code Agent**: Infrastructure, backend services, database operations, complex algorithms
- **Cursor Agent**: Testing frameworks, UI components, documentation, code polish
- **Coordination**: Handoff protocols, shared interface definitions, integration testing

### Excellence Flywheel Integration
```python
class ExcellenceFlywheel:
    """Enforce systematic methodology across all agents"""
    
    async def enforce_verification_first(self, agent: Agent, task: Task):
        # Mandatory pattern discovery before implementation
        patterns = await agent.discover_patterns(task.domain)
        verification = await agent.verify_current_state(task.scope)
        return self.validate_readiness(patterns, verification)
    
    async def quality_gate_check(self, agent: Agent, implementation: Implementation):
        # Verify consistency with established patterns
        return await self.architectural_consistency_check(implementation)
```

   - Monitor agent health and performance

### Phase 2: Excellence Flywheel Integration (4 points)

   - Architectural consistency validation
   - Quality gate implementation

2. **Kind Communication Wrapper**

   - Technical translation layer
   - Graceful error communication

3. **Progress Tracking and Monitoring**

   - Performance metrics collection

### Phase 3: Advanced Coordination (4 points)

   - Iterative improvement cycles

2. **Conflict Resolution and Merging**

## Description

Critical foundation repair required before PM-034 Phase 3 implementation can proceed. Root cause analysis revealed integration architecture failures causing 50% system health.

## Root Cause Analysis Summary

**System Health Status: 50% (2/4 components working)**

✅ **Working Components (Simple, Focused):**
- Query Response Formatter: 100% accuracy, 0.002ms performance
- Type System: Clean domain models, zero coupling

❌ **Failing Components (Complex, Stateful):**
- Database Connection: Session pattern inconsistency, transaction conflicts
- Slack Message Consolidation: Memory leaks, global state management issues

## Architecture Failures Identified

### 1. Database Session Management Pattern Conflict
- **Issue**: Two competing patterns (AsyncSessionFactory vs RepositoryFactory)
- **Evidence**: Transaction boundary conflicts in `services/database/repositories.py:49-115`
- **Impact**: Session leaks, connection pool exhaustion, integration failures

### 2. Slack Integration Over-Engineering
- **Issue**: 719-line response handler with global state management
- **Evidence**: `MESSAGE_CONSOLIDATION_BUFFER` and `PROCESSED_EVENTS` global dictionaries
- **Impact**: Memory leaks, race conditions, complex failure modes

### 3. Integration Layer Coupling
- **Issue**: 5-layer dependency chain with silent failure propagation
- **Evidence**: SpatialEvent → SlackContext → Intent → Orchestration → Response
- **Impact**: Mysterious integration breakdowns, poor error visibility

## Solution Framework (ADR-007)

### Phase 1: Database Session Unification
- [ ] Standardize on AsyncSessionFactory across all services
- [ ] Remove transaction boundary conflict logic from BaseRepository
- [ ] Eliminate RepositoryFactory and db.get_session() competing patterns
- [ ] Update core services to use session_scope() consistently
- [ ] Verify zero session leaks in integration tests

### Phase 2: Slack Integration Simplification  
- [ ] Replace MESSAGE_CONSOLIDATION_BUFFER with immediate response pattern
- [ ] Remove global state management (PROCESSED_EVENTS cleanup)
- [ ] Implement circuit breaker at integration boundaries
- [ ] Simplify response handler from 719 lines to focused single responsibility
- [ ] Verify memory stability under load

### Phase 3: Integration Health Monitoring
- [ ] Implement centralized error aggregation
- [ ] Add component health tracking with clear failure visibility
- [ ] Create graceful degradation strategies for failed components
- [ ] Establish clear separation of concerns between integration layers
- [ ] Verify 100% system health target

- [ ] Slack integration memory usage stable over 24 hours
- [ ] All services using unified session creation pattern

## Story
As a PM using Piper, I want to-do lists based on Universal Lists and To-Dos as core domain objects so I can create, manage, and track structured task lists with unlimited extensibility and AI assistance.

## Context
To-do lists are fundamental PM artifacts. We implemented a Universal List architecture that supports unlimited item types through composition over specialization, enabling sophisticated task management with zero future development overhead for new list types.

## Implementation Achieved (August 5, 2025)

### Universal List Architecture ✅
- **Composition Pattern**: `List(item_type='todo')` instead of specialized TodoList
- **Unlimited Extensibility**: `List(item_type='feature'|'bug'|'attendee'|'anything')`
- **Zero Breaking Changes**: Backward compatibility through wrapper classes
- **6-Minute Transformation**: Complete architectural refactoring in 6 minutes (3:45-3:51 PM)

### Domain Models Implemented ✅
```python
@dataclass
class List:
    """Universal List model for ANY item type (todo, feature, bug, attendee)"""
    id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid4()))
    name: str = ""
    item_type: str = "todo"  # Discriminator field
    list_type: str = "personal"
    # ... comprehensive model

@dataclass
class ListItem:
    """Universal ListItem relationship - polymorphic with item_type discriminator"""
    id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid4()))
    list_id: str = ""
    item_id: str = ""  # Polymorphic reference
    item_type: str = "todo"  # Discriminator
    # ... relationship management

@dataclass
class Todo:
    """Standalone Todo domain object - no coupling to lists"""
    id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid4()))
    title: str = ""
    description: str = ""
    status: str = "pending"
    # ... atomic todo object
```

### Database Implementation ✅
- **Universal Schema**: Single `lists` table supports all item types
- **Strategic Indexing**: 20+ optimized indexes for polymorphic queries
- **Data Migration**: Preserves existing data during transformation
- **Performance**: Efficient filtering by item_type discriminator

### Repository Layer ✅
- **UniversalListRepository**: Single implementation for all list types
- **Backward Compatibility**: TodoListRepository wrapper preserves existing APIs
- **Zero Breaking Changes**: All existing code continues to work
- **Performance Optimization**: Strategic query patterns for item_type filtering

## Technical Achievement Summary

### Code Efficiency ✅
- **55% Reduction**: 3,400+ specialized lines → 1,500+ universal lines
- **Unlimited Types**: Single implementation supports infinite item types
- **Future-Proof**: New types require zero additional development

### Architectural Excellence ✅
- **Composition over Specialization**: Fundamental design principle applied
- **Systematic Transformation**: Complete refactoring without quality compromise
- **Quality Preservation**: 100% backward compatibility maintained

### Strategic Impact ✅
- **Development Velocity**: New list types = 0 development time
- **Code Maintenance**: Single codebase vs N parallel systems
- **Extensibility**: `List(item_type='anything')` pattern proven

- [x] Basic CRUD operations working (UniversalListRepository)
- [x] Backward compatibility maintained (wrapper classes)
- [x] Zero breaking changes achieved

4. **Architectural Agility**: 6-minute transformation demonstrates rapid adaptation capability

## Story
As a user receiving responses from Piper Morgan in Slack, I want concise, single notifications for completed workflows instead of multiple verbose messages, so that my Slack channel remains clean and readable.

## Context
Following the successful completion of PM-078 (Slack Spatial Intelligence integration), Piper Morgan is now fully operational and responding to Slack mentions with complete end-to-end workflow execution. However, user testing revealed that workflow completion generates multiple verbose notifications:

- "🔔 Workflow completed successfully"  
- "✅ Task completed successfully"

3. ✅ Workflow details available on-demand (thread/reaction)
4. ✅ Clean, professional channel appearance

- [ ] Consolidate workflow completion into primary response
- [ ] Eliminate duplicate "task completed" notifications
- [ ] Preserve essential workflow information

[PM-063: Extend Graceful Degradation to QueryRouter](https://github.com/mediajunkie/piper-morgan-product/issues/47)

## Problem Statement

QUERY intents fail with 500 Internal Server Error when database is unavailable, while EXECUTION intents work perfectly through graceful degradation pattern.

**User Impact**:
- "Create GitHub issue" → ✅ Works without Docker
- "List all my projects" → ❌ 500 error without Docker

## Root Cause

QueryRouter requires database connectivity through AsyncSessionFactory, while OrchestrationEngine has test_mode pattern for database-independent operation.

**Current Architecture Gap**:
- ✅ OrchestrationEngine: Has test_mode parameter with graceful degradation
- ✅ Intent enrichment: Falls back to unenriched intent
- ✅ Workflow status: Falls back to in-memory workflows
- ❌ QueryRouter: No fallback, requires active database connection

## Proposed Solution

Extend the established test_mode pattern from OrchestrationEngine to QueryRouter:

1. **Pattern Consistency**: Add test_mode parameter to QueryRouter constructor
2. **Graceful Responses**: Return helpful messages when database unavailable
3. **User Transparency**: Clear feedback about degraded mode limitations
4. **Architectural Alignment**: Follow exact OrchestrationEngine approach

## Implementation Approach

**Phase 1: Pattern Extension**
- Add test_mode parameter to QueryRouter constructor
- Modify query methods to check test_mode before database access
- Implement graceful fallback responses

**Phase 2: Integration**
- Update main.py to pass test_mode when database unavailable
- Ensure consistent error handling across query types

**Phase 3: Testing**
- Test both database-available and degraded modes
- Verify user feedback clarity

## Chief Architect Guidance

- Follow OrchestrationEngine's test_mode pattern exactly
- Provide clear user feedback for degraded mode
- Avoid scattered database availability checks
- Be transparent about limited features

## Success Criteria

- [ ] "List projects" returns helpful message when database unavailable
- [ ] Pattern consistent with OrchestrationEngine approach
- [ ] Clear user feedback about degraded mode
- [ ] No scattered database checks
- [ ] Comprehensive test coverage for both modes

## Technical Context

Related to Activation & Polish Week goals and database-independent development workflow.

## Problem Statement
MCPResourceManager currently uses hybrid configuration approach that violates clean architecture principles:

```python
# Current problematic pattern in services/mcp/resources.py:82
if CONFIG_SERVICE_AVAILABLE:
    config = get_config()
    self.use_pool = config.pool_enabled and POOL_AVAILABLE
else:
    # Fallback to direct environment access
    self.use_pool = os.getenv("USE_MCP_POOL", "false").lower() == "true" and POOL_AVAILABLE
```

## Impact
- Test: `test_mcp_resource_manager_uses_configuration_service` failing (PM-015 Group 2)
- Mixed configuration patterns across codebase
- Direct environment variable access in business logic violates clean architecture
- Difficult to test configuration-dependent behavior consistently
- Configuration concerns scattered across multiple layers

## Current Status
**PM-015 Group 2 Results**: 21/23 MCP configuration tests passing (91% success rate)
This is one of 2 remaining architectural debt items blocking 100% test success.

## ADR Requirements
The ADR for this issue must address:

### 1. Configuration Access Pattern Decision
- **Option A**: Pure dependency injection (explicit, testable, SOLID principles)
- **Option B**: Service locator pattern (convenient, implicit dependencies)  
- **Option C**: Hybrid approach with clean abstractions (current refined)

### 2. Environment Variable Strategy
- **Option A**: Only at application bootstrap, never in business logic
- **Option B**: Through configuration service abstraction only
- **Option C**: Allow fallbacks but through structured interfaces

### 3. Migration Strategy
- Backward compatibility requirements for existing MCPResourceManager consumers
- Gradual vs. big-bang refactoring approach (recommend gradual)
- Timeline and implementation phases
- Impact on existing MCP integration points

### 4. Testing Strategy
- How to test configuration-dependent code consistently
- Mock configuration service vs. test configuration instances
- Integration with existing MCP test fixtures (PM-015 Group 2 improvements)

## Proposed Approach
Based on codebase analysis, recommended **3-phase gradual migration**:

### Phase 1: Clean Current Pattern
```python
# Eliminate direct os.getenv, use configuration service with proper fallback
from services.infrastructure.config.mcp_configuration import get_mcp_pool_enabled

class MCPResourceManager:
    def __init__(self, client_config: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None):
        # Clean configuration access - no direct os.getenv
        self.use_pool = get_mcp_pool_enabled() and POOL_AVAILABLE
```

### Phase 2: Optional Constructor Injection  
```python
class MCPResourceManager:
    def __init__(self, client_config=None, config_service=None):
        self.config_service = config_service or get_config_service()
        self.use_pool = self.config_service.pool_enabled and POOL_AVAILABLE
```

### Phase 3: Pure Dependency Injection
```python
class MCPResourceManager:
    def __init__(self, client_config: Dict[str, Any], config_service: MCPConfigurationService):
        # Explicit dependencies, fully testable
        self.config_service = config_service
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ADR created with architectural decision and detailed rationale
- [ ] Migration plan documented with clear phases and timeline
- [ ] Test `test_mcp_resource_manager_uses_configuration_service` passes ✅
- [ ] Pattern applied consistently across MCP components (MCPConnectionPool, etc.)
- [ ] Backward compatibility maintained during migration
- [ ] Documentation updated with new configuration patterns
- [ ] Integration with PM-015 Group 2 MCP test fixtures verified

- `tests/infrastructure/config/test_mcp_configuration.py:416` - Failing test
- `services/infrastructure/config/mcp_configuration.py` - Configuration service
- `services/infrastructure/mcp/connection_pool.py` - Similar pattern needing alignment

body:
## Problem Description

The test suite experiences AsyncPG connection pool concurrency issues when running database tests in batch. The error `cannot perform operation: another operation is in progress` occurs when multiple async operations attempt to use the same database connection simultaneously.

## Technical Details

### Symptoms
- Individual tests pass ✅
- Batch test execution fails ❌
- Specific error: `asyncpg.exceptions._base.InterfaceError: cannot perform operation: another operation is in progress`
- Issue occurs in `async_transaction` and `clean_database` fixtures in `conftest.py`

### Root Cause
AsyncPG connection pool contention occurs when:
1. Test fixtures (`async_transaction`, `clean_database`) create database sessions
2. Test methods simultaneously create their own sessions  
3. Multiple async operations try to use the same connection from the pool
4. AsyncPG driver doesn't allow concurrent operations on a single connection

### Affected Test Files
- `tests/test_file_repository_migration.py` - 6/8 tests fail in batch
- `tests/test_file_resolver_edge_cases.py` - 4/5 tests fail in batch
- `tests/test_file_scoring_weights.py` - Status unknown due to concurrency issues
- `tests/test_workflow_repository_migration.py` - Status unknown due to concurrency issues

### Current Workaround
Tests using `async_session` fixture (which uses `AsyncSessionFactory.session_scope()`) work correctly. The issue is specifically with the custom `async_transaction` fixture that attempts to provide transaction rollback isolation.

## Solution Options

### Option 1: Connection Pool Strategy Changes
- Implement per-test connection isolation
- Increase connection pool size with proper async coordination
- Use connection-per-fixture approach with careful lifecycle management

### Option 2: Database Driver Alternatives
- Evaluate asyncpg alternatives that handle concurrency better
- Consider using psycopg3 async driver
- Test with different SQLAlchemy async strategies

### Option 3: Test Synchronization 
- Implement fixture ordering to prevent concurrent database operations
- Use async locks/semaphores to coordinate database access
- Redesign fixture dependencies to eliminate overlap

### Option 4: Alternative Testing Strategy
- Use database transactions with rollback for test isolation
- Implement database snapshots/restore between tests
- Consider using an in-memory database for tests

## Impact Assessment

**Current Status**: PM-015 Group 1 test infrastructure fixes are 80% complete
- ✅ Fixed pytest collection errors
- ✅ Fixed missing database tables  
- ✅ Moved problematic test files
- ❌ AsyncPG concurrency blocking batch test execution

**Priority**: Medium-High
- Blocks effective test-driven development workflow
- Prevents reliable CI/CD test execution
- Impacts developer productivity with testing

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All Group 1 tests pass in batch execution
- [x] `async_transaction` fixture works reliably for transaction rollback testing
- [x] Connection pool concurrency issues eliminated
- [x] Documentation updated with new testing patterns
- [x] Performance impact of solution is acceptable (< 2x slower)

## Related Issues

- PM-015: Test Infrastructure Isolation Fix (parent issue)
- Related to AsyncSessionFactory pattern implementation

## Files Requiring Changes

- `conftest.py` - Core fixture definitions
- `services/database/session_factory.py` - Session management  
- `services/database/connection.py` - Database connection pool
- Test files using `async_transaction` fixture

body:
**Story:** As a development team, we need automated validation of domain/database consistency to prevent drift bugs

**Description:**
We've had multiple bugs from domain/database drift. This ticket is to create a tool that programmatically compares SQLAlchemy models with our domain dataclasses to enforce the "Domain-First" architectural pattern.

**Estimate:** 3-5 points | **Sprint:** Foundation & Cleanup Sprint - Week 2

**Implementation Details:**
- Create tools/check_domain_db_consistency.py script
- Compare field names between domain and database models
- Integrate into CI/CD pipeline with build failure on mismatch
- Provide migration hints when mismatches found

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A new script is created (e.g., `tools/check_domain_db_consistency.py`)
- [ ] The script compares field names between domain models (e.g., `Workflow`) and their corresponding database models (e.g., `WorkflowDB`)
- [ ] The script is integrated into the CI/CD pipeline
- [ ] The build fails if a mismatch is detected
- [ ] The script's output provides hints for creating a new migration if a mismatch is found

**Implementation Hint:**
```python
# tools/check_domain_db_consistency.py
import dataclasses
from services.domain.models import Workflow
from services.database.models import WorkflowDB

def check_consistency():
    domain_fields = {f.name for f in dataclasses.fields(Workflow)}
    db_fields = {c.name for c in WorkflowDB.__table__.columns}
    
    missing_in_domain = db_fields - domain_fields
    missing_in_db = domain_fields - db_fields
    
    if missing_in_domain or missing_in_db:
        # Custom error class would be ideal
        raise Exception(
            f"Domain/DB mismatch found\!\n"
            f"Fields in DB but not Domain: {missing_in_domain}\n"
            f"Fields in Domain but not DB: {missing_in_db}"
        )
```

**Architectural Insight:**
These bugs reveal that our Domain-First Database Pattern needs stronger enforcement. The pattern is correct, but we lack tooling to ensure the database actually follows the domain. This tool will close that gap.

**Related Issues:** Part of Foundation & Cleanup Sprint

**Labels:** `sprint-2`, `engineering`, `tooling`
title: PM-056: Create domain/database schema validator

## Story
As a PM managing multiple projects, I want Piper Morgan to intelligently track and switch between projects/repositories based on context

## Description
Implement project-centric architecture where each project can have multiple integrations (GitHub repos, Jira boards, etc.). The system should intelligently determine which project the user is working with based on:

- Last used project in session
- Context from user messages
- Explicit project references
- Sensible defaults

Projects become first-class domain objects with their own integrations, settings, and knowledge contexts.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ]  Project domain model with integration support (GitHub, future: Jira, Slack)
- [ ]  Intelligent project resolution following hierarchy: explicit → last used → inferred → default
- [ ]  Session-based project memory (remembers last project used)
- [ ]  Confirmation flow when project selection is ambiguous
- [ ]  List available projects on request
- [ ]  Error handling for missing projects or integrations
- [ ]  Projects stored in PostgreSQL with proper constraints
- [ ]  Workflows automatically include project context
- [ ]  GitHub integration uses project-specific repository and labels

## Technical Requirements

- Domain-first implementation (Project as core business entity)
- IntegrationType enum in shared_types.py
- ProjectContext service for centralized resolution logic
- Repository pattern for all database operations
- Support future cross-project workflows (data model prepared)

## Estimate
8 points (increased from 5 due to added intelligence and error handling)

## Status
In Development

## Dependencies
PM-003 (completed)

# Test Coverage
Comprehensive test-first implementation covering:

- Project resolution hierarchy
- Ambiguous project handling
- Integration validation
- Error scenarios
- Future cross-project support
